Computation Pipeline¶

This page describes the internal four-phase pipeline that executes when you call fkcompute.fk(). Understanding the pipeline helps you choose the right performance knobs and debug unexpected results.

Overview¶

A call to fkcompute.fk() with a braid word and degree passes through four sequential phases before returning the result dictionary.

Phase 0 — Parameter setup. Input parameters are normalised, presets are merged, and file-saving paths are resolved.

Phase 1 — Sign assignment (inversion). A valid \(\pm 1\) sign assignment for the braid strands is determined. For homogeneous braids this is immediate; for others a parallel search over sign-assignment candidates is performed.

Phase 2 — ILP generation. The braid topology and sign assignment are translated into a reduced constraint system, validated for feasibility by Gurobi, and written to a .csv file for the C++ backend.

Phase 3 — C++ computation. The compiled fk_main binary reads the .csv file and evaluates the FK state sum, accumulating the polynomial coefficients using arbitrary-precision arithmetic.

Phase 4 — Assembly and formatting. Python reads the JSON output of the binary, attaches metadata (braid used, sign assignment, component count), optionally converts the result to a symbolic SymPy expression, and returns the final dictionary.

Phase 0 — Parameter Setup¶

Before any computation, the fkcompute.fk() function normalises parameters:

  • Applies the selected preset ("single thread" or "parallel") by merging its values into the parameter dict.

  • Explicit keyword arguments take precedence over preset values.

  • Sets up file-saving paths if save_data=True.

Relevant source: src/fkcompute/api/presets.py.

Skip logic: if you provide an ilp_file, Phases 1 and 2 are skipped entirely. If you provide an inversion dict or inversion_file, only Phase 1 is skipped.

Phase 1 — Sign Assignment (Inversion)¶

Goal: find a valid \(\pm 1\) sign assignment for each strand segment of the braid so that the R-matrix equations are satisfiable.

Entry point: fkcompute.inversion.api.find_sign_assignment().

Braid Type Detection¶

The function first classifies the braid:

  • Homogeneous: every braid generator \(\sigma_k\) appears with a consistent sign throughout the word. The sign assignment is then determined analytically and computed immediately — no search required.

  • Non-homogeneous (fibered): at least one generator appears with mixed signs. A parallel search is performed.

Parallel Search (Non-Homogeneous Braids)¶

The search, implemented in fkcompute.inversion.search.parallel_try_sign_assignments(), proceeds as follows:

  1. Braid variants — cyclic shifts and (optionally) horizontal reflections of the braid word are generated by fkcompute.inversion.variants.generate_braid_variants().

  2. Permutation candidates — for each variant, a set of sign-assignment candidates is derived from the sign diagram of the braid (a combinatorial structure encoding which strand-crossing patterns are compatible). Candidates are generated by fkcompute.inversion.permutations.iter_perms_rot_closed().

  3. Parallel evaluation — candidates are distributed across Python worker processes (max_workers) in chunks of chunk_size. Each worker tests its batch by calling the constraint reducer and the Gurobi ILP checker.

  4. Early exit — as soon as one worker finds a valid assignment, the remaining workers are cancelled.

Performance knobs:

Parameter

Effect

max_workers

Number of Python processes. Set to cpu_count for maximum throughput.

chunk_size

Candidates per task. Smaller values increase responsiveness to an early hit; larger values reduce inter-process overhead.

include_flip

Whether to include reflected braid variants. Roughly doubles the search space; may help for certain knot types.

max_shifts

Cap on the number of cyclic shifts tried.

Phase 2 — Constraint System and ILP Generation¶

Goal: translate the braid topology and sign assignment into a constraint system that the C++ backend can use.

Entry point: internal helper _generate_ilp in src/fkcompute/api/compute.py.

Steps:

  1. Relation generation — fkcompute.domain.braid.states.BraidStates.get_state_relations() generates the full list of constraints (equalities, inequalities, conservation laws) implied by the R-matrix structure.

  2. Constraint reduction — fkcompute.domain.constraints.reduction.full_reduce() applies fixed-point propagation: alias substitution, constant propagation, and variable elimination. This dramatically reduces the number of free variables.

  3. Symbolic assignment — the reduced system is solved symbolically, expressing each state in terms of a small number of independent integer variables (the “free variables”).

  4. ILP feasibility check — Gurobi verifies that the system has at least one bounded integer solution. This step catches degenerate cases before invoking the expensive C++ computation.

  5. ILP file generation — fkcompute.solver.ilp.ilp() writes the constraint data to a .csv file in a custom format expected by the C++ backend.

Phase 3 — C++ Computation¶

Goal: evaluate the FK state sum and accumulate polynomial coefficients.

Entry point: fkcompute.infra.binary.run_fk_binary().

The Python layer executes the fk_main binary as a subprocess:

fk_main <ilp_base> <output_base> --threads N

Binary resolution:

  1. If fk_main is on PATH, that binary is used.

  2. Otherwise the packaged binary at fkcompute/_bin/fk_main is used.

Inside the binary:

  • Reads the .csv constraint file produced in Phase 2.

  • Uses OpenMP to parallelise the outer loop over state assignments.

  • Accumulates the FK polynomial using FLINT arbitrary-precision arithmetic.

  • Writes the result to <output_base>.json.

See C++ Backend for details on the C++ architecture.

Phase 4 — Assembly and Formatting¶

After the binary finishes, Python:

  1. Reads <output_base>.json.

  2. Augments metadata with:

    • braid — the braid word actually used.

    • inversion — the sign assignment found in Phase 1.

    • components — number of link components (from fkcompute.domain.braid.states.BraidStates).

  3. If symbolic=True and SymPy is available, computes and attaches a pretty-printed polynomial string as metadata["symbolic"].

  4. If save_data=False (the default), deletes the intermediate _inversion.json, _ilp.csv, and .json files.

  5. Returns the result dictionary to the caller.

Progress Callbacks (Interactive Mode)¶

When the interactive wizard is active, the internal fkcompute.api.compute._fk_compute() function accepts a private _progress_callback argument — a fkcompute.interactive.progress.FKProgressTracker instance.

The tracker receives start/update/complete events from each phase and renders live progress panels in the terminal using Rich.
